Badger proved that contiguous, immutable columnar data can deliver massive performance gains, motivating a more durable redesign.
Arrow provides zero-copy, columnar interoperability across systems, solving the costly data interchange bottleneck.
A future Arrow-based analytics engine with query planning and multicore execution is needed to eliminate pandas’s temporary allocations and single-core limits.
Dask helps with scale but cannot fix pandas’s underlying memory and IO inefficiencies because it treats pandas as a black box.
money_quotes: Nowadays, my rule of thumb for pandas is that you should have 5 to 10 times as much RAM as the size of your dataset.
It’s an overall bad situation for large datasets.
Zero-copy data access was the biggest thing; you need to be able to memory map complex tables to make accessing 1 terabyte of data on disk as fast and easy as 1 megabyte.
I strongly feel that Arrow is a key technology for the next generation of data science tools.
In Arrow, each string is right next to the previous one in memory, so you can scan all of the data in a column of strings without any cache misses.
pandas’s lack of a C or Cython API for data frames has been another big problem over time.
Dask makes it easy to read a directory of CSV files by running pandas.read_csv in parallel and then running a groupby operation on the entire dataset.
dask.dataframe does not solve pandas’s inherent performance and memory use problems, but it spreads them out across multiple processes.
posts.key_insight: Pandas’s architectural debt makes large-scale analytics painful, and Arrow’s zero-copy columnar infrastructure is the long-term path to interoperable, high-performance data tooling.

posts.summary: McKinney explains that Feather began as a minimal Arrow-based file format to enable fast, shared data-frame exchange between Python and R, and that it has now moved into the Apache Arrow codebase. He argues this consolidation simplifies implementation and lets Feather inherit ongoing Arrow performance improvements. Feather remains a lightweight subset of Arrow with constraints around types, batching, and nesting, but those gaps can be closed by reusing Arrow’s richer stream format and metadata. The move has already yielded substantial speedups for Python users due to multithreaded conversions and more efficient pandas construction. The main blocker to further feature evolution is the lack of Arrow C++ bindings for R, after which parity and new capabilities should accelerate.
key_points: Feather was created as a minimal Arrow-aligned format to bridge Python and R data frames quickly before Arrow’s full formats matured.
Feather’s limitations (limited types, single batch, no nested data) are largely solvable by transitioning to Arrow stream formats.
Porting Feather into the Arrow codebase simplifies the implementation and directly benefits from Arrow’s performance work.
Python users already see major speed gains from multithreaded Arrow↔pandas conversions and better DataFrame construction.
Progress on R support is gated by missing Arrow C++ bindings, which are required for feature parity and future expansion.
money_quotes: Feather files are Arrow memory on disk (and thus support zero-copy access), but have more limited metadata.

Moving Feather development to the Arrow codebase has made the implementation substantially simpler, and Feather users immediately benefit from any performance improvements or general Arrow-related development work.
Perhaps amazingly, Feather 0.4.0 based on pyarrow 0.7.1 is much faster than Feather 0.3.1.
Feather 0.3.1 can read a 50-column DataFrame at about 1.6 GB/s while Feather 0.4.0 ... can read at about 2.9 GB/s, a reduction in runtime of over 40%.
Until R bindings for Apache Arrow ship, it will be difficult to innovate on Feather's feature set.
I'm excited to engage with the R community on this work and look forward to delivering more Apache Arrow-based IO and computation tools to both the R and Python communities.
posts.key_insight: By consolidating Feather inside Apache Arrow, the format gains simpler internals and accelerating performance, but its next wave of features depends on Arrow’s R bindings.

posts.summary: McKinney argues that Apache Arrow is not a third columnar storage format competing with Parquet or ORC, but a complementary in-memory runtime representation. He explains that storage formats require decoding and often proprietary in-memory layouts, which makes algorithms non-portable across systems. Arrow standardizes the in-memory structure so decoded data can be processed zero-copy and shared across processes without serialization overhead. He disputes misconceptions about Arrow’s compression support and batch-size limits, noting these are resource and design choices rather than fundamental constraints. The conclusion is optimistic: Arrow enables cross-pollination between database and data science communities, and continued investment will unlock broader performance and interoperability gains.
key_points: Arrow targets in-memory runtime representation, while Parquet/ORC target persistent storage; they are complementary, not competitors.
Standardized in-memory layouts remove the need for system-specific formats and make algorithms portable across environments.
Zero-copy access enables efficient data sharing across processes, memory-mapped files, and network transport with minimal overhead.
Compression limitations are due to engineering bandwidth, not lack of awareness; broader schemes are possible and useful for some use cases.
Arrow does not impose a 64K record batch limit; batches can be arbitrarily sized.
money_quotes: Arrow is not competing with Parquet and ORC.
Arrow is an ephemeral, runtime in-memory representation that can be processed without any overhead on a per array-cell basis.
What Arrow provides a Parquet or ORC columnar storage user is a standardized in-memory data structure to place decoded data.
If you don't use something standardized, in many cases you will have to roll your own "proprietary", non-portable data structures.
One of the most game-changing aspects of Apache Arrow, is its role in data access and data movement.
That we have not implemented these compression schemes is not a function of our ignorance, but rather our available time and engineering resources.
We must walk before we can run.
As more pieces of Arrow fall into place, and more integrations in other projects get built, things are going to get very exciting for all involved.
posts.key_insight: Arrow’s value is in standardizing in-memory data so systems can share and process data zero-copy, making it a complementary runtime layer rather than a competing storage format.

posts.summary: The post argues that open source data science needs durable funding models that support both maintenance and high-risk innovation. McKinney contrasts the easier-to-justify economics of maintaining critical infrastructure with the harder case for funding new breakthroughs, drawing on pandas’ early unpaid development as evidence. He outlines common funding “traps” (consulting, startup, corporate user) that distract or destabilize project stewardship. His experience across DataPad, Cloudera, Two Sigma, and the growth of Apache Arrow leads him to conclude that cross-language interoperability is the core long-term challenge. Ursa Labs is presented as an independent lab designed to build shared data infrastructure, with RStudio and Two Sigma providing operational and strategic support. The conclusion is a call for sustained, collaborative investment in a portable, Arrow-based runtime that serves multiple languages and communities.
key_points: Open source projects oscillate between innovation and maintenance, and each phase requires different funding incentives and support structures.
Innovation funding is structurally harder but essential; without it, productivity, efficiency, and openness in data science will stall.
Common funding paths can become traps that dilute focus or create governance conflicts for OSS maintainers.
Apache Arrow enables shared, cross-language infrastructure, making interoperability a central technical and organizational goal.
Ursa Labs is created to provide independent, sustained capacity for building data science systems beyond any single company’s scope.
money_quotes: Funding open source software development is a complicated subject.
In my experience, open source projects feature two dominant archetypal modes: innovation and maintenance.
OSS maintainers, which are often volunteers, routinely “burn out” under the strain of supporting burgeoning user bases who sometimes take a project’s existence and maintenance for granted.
If we had refused to build pandas unless we raised enough money to pay for our rent and families’ cost of living, the project likely would not be what it is today.

As Apache Arrow has gotten off the ground over the last few years, it has become apparent that the problems we are tackling are much larger in scope than the interests of a single organization or even programming language.
I envision a portable, community-standard “data science runtime” that can be utilized for processing native Arrow-based data frames in just about any programming language.
The reality is that Hadley and I think the “language wars” are stupid when the real problem we are solving is human user interface design for data analysis.
We are only now at the beginning of a long journey ahead of us to advance the state of the art in data science tools.
posts.key_insight: Sustaining data science innovation requires independent, cross-language infrastructure investment, and Ursa Labs is positioned to build that Arrow-based foundation beyond the limits of any single organization.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney reflects on a decade of open-source work, using pandas as his entry point, to examine how open source and data science co-evolved. He contrasts industry-led projects with community-led ones, noting speed and resource advantages for the former but fragility when corporate priorities shift. Community-led projects are more resilient and transparent, yet they suffer from burnout, underinvestment in maintenance, and unreliable funding. He argues that myths about organic growth and developer replaceability obscure the true cost of sustaining critical infrastructure. He concludes with a call to contribute time or money, highlighting Apache governance and the creation of Ursa Labs to fund Apache Arrow.
key_points: Open source and data science advanced together because reuse and collaboration lower the cost of solving complex problems.
Industry-led projects can move fast but are vulnerable to corporate strategy changes; community-led projects are slower but more robust.
Maintenance work is undervalued and hard to fund, even though project health depends on it.
Developer burnout and loss of institutional memory are real risks, making maintainers hard to replace.
Transparent, consensus-driven governance (e.g., ASF) builds trust and long-term resilience.
money_quotes: The best code is the code that you don't have to write.
There's 15 people who are essentially making these three open-source projects that we all depend on work, which is sort of terrifying.
I actually had an email thread one time where a pandas user described himself as a customer of the project.
If developers burn out, it can make a project significantly weaker.
A lot of open-source maintainers carry so much project history and knowledge in their heads that it would take an individual months or years to be effective.
The two most significant sources of funding for open-source, the number one is self-funding, so no funding.
If every pandas user gave $1 per year, that would be a lot of money and could allow a lot of people to spend work full time working on pandas.
One of the reasons I like community-led projects is that there are fewer single points of failure.
posts.key_insight: Open-source success depends less on innovation hype and more on sustainable funding, governance, and maintenance that prevent burnout and preserve institutional knowledge.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney argues that open standards are essential for reducing fragmentation, enabling interoperability, and making code reuse practical across data systems. He explains that tabular data formats are currently siloed across languages and databases, which prevents algorithms from being shared and forces costly conversions. Apache Arrow addresses this by defining a language-independent columnar in-memory format that enables zero-copy data sharing and more efficient computation. He highlights technical advantages of columnar layouts, including cache-friendly access patterns, SIMD vectorization, and compression. The project’s growth, multi-language support, and real-world integrations demonstrate Arrow’s role as a broader development platform, not just a spec. He concludes that defragmenting the ecosystem through shared libraries and standards is critical to long-term open source success and productivity.
key_points: Open standards simplify architectures, reduce fragmentation, and enable cross-system code reuse.
Columnar memory layouts unlock major performance gains through cache locality and vectorization.
Arrow solves the N-squared interoperability problem by providing a common in-memory table format.
Arrow has matured into a cross-language development platform with industry adoption and zero-copy interoperability.
Sustaining this ecosystem requires coordinated open source funding and collaboration across communities.
money_quotes: CSV is not an open standard despite what anyone might tell you.
If an R programmer writes an algorithm that runs against an R data frame, I can't take that code and run it against a pandas data frame except in very rare cases.

Going from an algorithm that has a lot of cache misses to one that doesn't could mean a factor of 10 or 100 times performance increase in an algorithm even though you might be comparing one linear time algorithm to another.
So big O doesn't help you, it really comes down to your memory access patterns.
The idea of the Arrow project at its outset was to define an open standard for column oriented tables, so data frames, that's language independent.
This thorny problem of JVM interop between the JVM world and the native code world is essentially solved by the tools that we've developed in this project, assuming your data is tabular.

I think given how difficult it is to produce open source software, the more that we can defragment the ecosystem and work together to build reusable libraries and portable systems, it will make all of us a great deal more productive and successful.
posts.key_insight: Arrow’s core value is a shared, columnar memory standard that enables zero-copy interoperability and reusable tooling across languages, which is essential for long-term, sustainable data infrastructure.

posts.summary: Wes argues that data systems are fragmented because in-memory formats, APIs, and tooling are tightly coupled and lack shared standards. He presents Apache Arrow as a cross-language, columnar in-memory format that enables zero-copy data sharing and reusable computation across ecosystems like Python and R. He connects this technical goal to open source sustainability, describing Ursa Labs as a nonprofit effort to fund shared infrastructure and collaboration between communities. He explains that Arrow supports higher-performance analytics by enabling vectorized, batch execution rather than slow row-by-row interfaces. The conclusion is a vision of deconstructed, modular data systems and “data microservices” with standardized, high-speed access that reduce friction for data scientists.
key_points: Fragmented, vertically integrated data systems inhibit interoperability and reuse of algorithms across languages.
Standardizing in-memory formats enables zero-copy sharing and broader code reuse, similar to longstanding array standards.
Arrow provides a columnar format optimized for analytics and supports both CPU and GPU workflows.
Vectorized execution via Arrow-based interchange can deliver massive speedups (e.g., 100x) even with serialization steps.
Long-term progress requires sustainable open source funding and cross-community collaboration.
money_quotes: If you think about open source software as a public good in the sense that we have clean water and roads and sewers, maybe we should take the same approach to essentially individuals, companies and governments funding development of open source infrastructure.

The two things I'm most interested in is sharing data without having to copy or serialize and sharing code, so being able to take an algorithm that somebody else wrote and being able to run it on my data without having to rewrite the algorithm.
Open source communities are fragmented by their nature. It's sort of an anarchy. There's no central government making decisions.
I wanted to create the environment that would enable collaboration between the data science world and the database systems world.

Our goal is to kind of deconstruct the analytic database to break things into libraries that have public APIs that are reusable and you can pick and choose the components that you want to use to build a data processing system.

We really envision a future of data microservices, where you have a centralized metadata registry, and you can see all the data services available, use the exact same client interface to access all of them, and push and pull datasets at high speed.
posts.key_insight: Standardizing in-memory data with Apache Arrow enables interoperable, high-performance analytics across languages while supporting a modular, sustainable data ecosystem.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney announces NVIDIA AI Labs as a supporter of Ursa Labs, highlighting RAPIDS’ adoption of Apache Arrow for interoperable tabular data. He argues that Arrow exists to make data and algorithms shareable across languages and environments with minimal cost. In GPU analytics, serialization and copying can outweigh compute, so a common in-memory format is essential. Arrow’s columnar layout aligns well with CUDA’s execution model, making it a strong fit for GPU analytics. He describes recent efforts to engage the GPGPU community, including contributions and a CUDA Arrow extension. The post concludes with optimism about the ecosystem’s growth and a call for additional sponsors.
key_points: NVIDIA’s support strengthens Ursa Labs and aligns RAPIDS with Apache Arrow’s interoperability goals.
Zero-copy data sharing is critical; without it, crossing languages or environments becomes costly.
Common in-memory structures are required to share algorithms, not just data.
Arrow’s columnar format is well-suited for GPU analytics due to CUDA’s manycore model.
The Arrow community is expanding into GPGPU through concrete implementations and tooling.
money_quotes: NVIDIA's new open source RAPIDS data science platform uses Apache Arrow for an interoperable representation of tabular data (data frames).
If you can't share data at zero cost, then crossing languages or computation environments incurs a high penalty
You generally cannot share algorithms (code) without utilizing common in-memory data structures
The Arrow columnar format is an ideal fit for in-memory analytics on GPUs.
The cost of serialization or memory copying can frequently exceed that of the underlying GPU kernel execution.
We now have a CUDA Arrow extension library in C++ (with Python bindings) for managing interprocess communication and CUDA device memory management.
posts.key_insight: Apache Arrow’s zero-copy, columnar in-memory standard is essential for interoperable GPU analytics, and NVIDIA’s backing signals growing momentum for that ecosystem.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney reflects on a decade of building open source data tools, framing collaboration as a higher‑order need enabled only after basic necessities are met. He argues that the hardest part of open source is not the code but the human work of aligning motivations and sustaining collaboration. He recounts pandas’ origins, the early struggle against fragmentation, and the pragmatic focus on solving core workflows like reliable CSV ingestion. The limits of pandas’ vertical integration and performance needs in real products pushed him toward a broader interoperability vision. Apache Arrow is presented as the technical and social bridge to unify data science, databases, and big data systems, but he emphasizes the long horizons and funding challenges. He closes optimistic that cross‑community collaboration will reduce fragmentation and unlock better use of modern hardware.
key_points: Open source success depends more on collaboration and shared incentives than on engineering difficulty.
Fragmentation across data tools and formats is a systemic drag that prevents reliable end‑to‑end workflows.
Pandas’ vertically integrated design enabled rapid growth but created long‑term maintenance, performance, and bit‑rot risks.
Apache Arrow aims to provide a language‑independent data layer to reuse algorithms and bridge ecosystems.
Sustaining ambitious open source requires new funding models and longer time horizons.
money_quotes: How can I make myself more productive? How can I make people I work with more productive? How can I make strangers on the internet more productive?

The technical side of open source, the engineering, the code that you write, is probably the easy part... collaborating with people, understanding other people's motivations and communicating at a level where you're able to create a productive collaboration, that is very difficult.
I've been sort of jumping from one existential crisis to another.
Fragmentation is killing us.
If there is a killer app for pandas, it's pandas read CSV.
When you own all of this code, and often with a small team, the code can go out of date pretty quickly, and bit rot is an issue.
My goal with this project ultimately is to put an embedded analytic database at your fingertips.
The longer the horizon of building the thing that you want, the harder it becomes to get funding and support.
posts.key_insight: Open source data tooling advances fastest when ecosystems reduce fragmentation through shared, interoperable foundations—and when communities solve the human and funding challenges that make long‑term collaboration possible.

posts.summary: McKinney explains his decision to leave New York City for Nashville after years split between NYC and San Francisco, framing it as a values-driven lifestyle change. He argues that major tech hubs extract talent for profit, which clashes with the mission of open source work. High cost of living, tax burdens, and the stress of maintaining basic amenities make NYC and the Bay Area untenable for many, especially families. Open source work offers geographic freedom, but sustaining it requires more affordable, supportive communities. He concludes that cities like Nashville can enable healthier lives for developers and help decentralize tech ecosystems.
key_points: Profit-driven corporate ecosystems in NYC/SF conflict with the ethos of open source development.
Extreme living costs and taxes create constant pressure and limit quality of life.
Open source offers geographic flexibility, enabling distributed teams outside major hubs.
Sustaining full-time open source work requires building affordable tech communities.
Nashville combines affordability, connectivity, and livability without the downsides of a mega-hub.
money_quotes: I've increasingly felt that open source development is at odds with the values that are driving a large portion of the corporate world, particularly in the United States.
Non-wealthy people like myself in NYC are hyper-aware that they are part of the "underclass".
Even if one is not concerned with improving their standard of living, just maintaining the current standard of living can be pretty stressful.
Rather than pouring money down the drain on NYC living expenses, I would rather use the money to create a more comfortable and productive environment for myself to do open source work.
If we are to enable more people to do open source development as a full-time vocation, we need to grow supportive tech communities in places that are more affordable.
It shouldn't be that software engineers should have to sacrifice their quality of life to be able to work on open source projects that they feel passionately about.
We need to grow vibrant communities of entrepreneurs and technologists in other parts of the US.
posts.key_insight: To make open source a sustainable career, developers need to build and support affordable tech communities beyond the traditional coastal hubs.

posts.summary: In this PyData Miami talk, Wes McKinney announces Ursa Labs, a nonprofit aimed at sustaining Apache Arrow and shared data infrastructure across languages. He argues that pandas and much of data science tooling sit atop legacy assumptions from 2007-era tech and earlier, which breaks down for multicore, GPU, and out-of-core workloads. Arrow’s core goal is a standard, immutable in-memory columnar format that enables zero-copy data sharing and reusable algorithms across ecosystems. He highlights concrete progress: Arrow Flight RPC, LLVM-based expression compilation, GPU data frames, and faster Spark pandas UDFs via Arrow’s memory layout. The conclusion is that durable, interoperable foundations can reduce fragmentation, accelerate performance, and make data science workflows simpler and more sustainable over the long term.
key_points: Open source sustainability is getting harder, motivating Ursa Labs as a nonprofit to fund long-term infrastructure work.
Pandas’ eager evaluation and Python-object string storage are inefficient for large-scale, modern hardware realities.
Arrow provides a language-agnostic, in-memory standard to eliminate repeated data conversions and enable reusable algorithms.
Zero-copy interoperability across JVM, native code, and GPUs unlocks new performance and system architectures.
Future vision includes Arrow-native data services and a next-generation data frame for large, disk-backed datasets.
money_quotes: "It's getting harder and harder to build open source software sustainably."

"We kind of want to get away from this world where we're having to do all of this conversion between point A and point B all the time and at least have some kind of a way of representing data at the memory level."
"Even an empty string in Python 3 takes up 48 bytes."
"If the data is an immutable standard, then if you have two algorithms which know how to interact with arrow memory, then they don't have to be rewritten."
"It's kind of always been the dream, and my dream, to be able to have zero-copy data sharing of complex data sets between the JVM and the native code world."
"I think it is sort of sad that pandas wasn't really designed for working with massive data sets."

"As a data scientist, data scientists are overwhelmed with all of the complexity and things that people have to worry about just to deal with, I want to read a data set, I want to perform some simple analytics and feature engineering and then use TensorFlow."
posts.key_insight: A shared, zero-copy in-memory standard like Arrow is the linchpin for sustainable, interoperable, and scalable data tooling beyond pandas-era constraints.

posts.summary: The report chronicles Ursa Labs’ January 2019 work around the Apache Arrow 0.12 release and the maintenance burden that comes with a rapidly expanding project. It highlights a broad roadmap centered on Flight RPC, C++ kernels for a future query engine, and performance and format improvements in Parquet and JSON ingestion. The post details cross-language progress in C++, Python, and R, with packaging and distribution challenges called out as major friction points. It also emphasizes investments in build infrastructure and community growth as necessary to scale development. The conclusion is a forward-looking status update: Arrow is growing quickly, but sustaining that growth requires better tooling, packaging, and shared community effort.
key_points: Arrow 0.12 shipped amid significant build and packaging firefighting, signaling operational strain from rapid project growth.
Near-term priorities focus on Flight RPC, C++ kernels for an Arrow-native query engine, and Parquet/nested type support.
Cross-language work (Python, R, C++) centers on performance, better I/O, and difficult packaging standards like manylinux1 and CRAN.
Infrastructure investments (DGX stations, build cluster automation) are positioned as productivity multipliers for the community.
Community momentum is strong, with rising contributors and new initiatives like benchmarking databases and a donated Rust query engine.
money_quotes: After a high-intensity 3 months of development, we helped release Apache Arrow 0.12 on January 20th.
A good chunk of our time was spent fighting fires (in packaging and builds) related to the continued expansion of the project in recent months.
Hardening the new Flight RPC system for production and making it available to C++, Python, and Java developers is a major area of upcoming development interest.
In Python we are working with the Ray, TensorFlow, and PyTorch communities to resolve some packaging issues related to the manylinux1 standard for wheel binary packages.
We are working on a plan to get Arrow into CRAN to make it easier for R users to install the software.
Our build cluster is growing and we intend to use this hardware to make the Apache Arrow community more productive.
As Apache Arrow approaches its third birthday as a top-level Apache project, we have surpassed 3000 stars on GitHub with over 240 unique contributors.
posts.key_insight: Arrow’s rapid growth is driving both ambitious feature work and a rising need for better packaging, tooling, and infrastructure to keep the ecosystem sustainable.

posts.summary: The post is a monthly progress report from Ursa Labs focused on advancing Apache Arrow toward the 0.13 release. It argues that Arrow’s community momentum and technical roadmap are strong, emphasizing growing adoption of the columnar format and cross-language platform. The report highlights concrete engineering investments—like a physical build/test cluster and new performance benchmarking—to improve reliability and developer productivity. It details key technical initiatives such as JSON ingestion, Arrow Flight, dataset frameworks, computational kernels, Gandiva packaging, and extension types. The conclusion is forward-looking: the team will prioritize release readiness, packaging support, and new data types while continuing to deepen the Arrow ecosystem.
key_points: Arrow’s community is growing quickly, and the project’s third birthday marks increasing adoption of its columnar format and cross-language platform.
Ursa Labs is investing in infrastructure (a multi-machine build/test cluster) to enable nightly testing, packaging, and benchmarking.
Major engineering work focuses on Arrow Flight, dataset frameworks, computational kernels, and JSON ingestion as building blocks for in-memory query systems.
Packaging and portability remain critical, with efforts to ship Gandiva and integrate Flight into conda and Python wheels.
Release planning for 0.13 drives near-term priorities, including new data types and automation of benchmarks.
money_quotes: The Apache Arrow community is discussing a 0.13 release toward the end of March, so we spent February helping the project toward the next release milestone.
We’re really looking forward to what the next few years will bring as the Arrow columnar format and cross-language development platform becomes even more widely adopted.
One significant project not represented in the Apache Arrow open source project is setting up a physical build and test cluster for Ursa Labs.
We believe this will form the backbone of future distributed systems powered by Apache Arrow.
This is an essential component for general-purpose in-memory query processing.
To lay the foundations for an Arrow-native in-memory query engine, we have been implementing aggregation functions to enable parallel aggregation of Arrow datasets.
In March one of our main priorities will be working with the Arrow community to get the 0.13 release out the door.
posts.key_insight: The report frames Arrow’s rapid ecosystem growth as inseparable from disciplined infrastructure work—build/test automation, packaging, and core feature development—needed to ship a stable 0.13 release.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney recounts how his early experience with Python’s expressiveness and later frustrations with clumsy data tools pushed him to build more productive analytics software. He argues that user-facing tools like pandas are constrained by underlying systems, motivating his shift toward infrastructure work such as Apache Arrow. He outlines how fragmented big-data ecosystems and weak interoperability create unnecessary friction, and why a common, language-independent data representation is essential. He also details the sustainability challenges of open-source funding, emphasizing the undervalued maintenance work that keeps projects healthy. The conclusion is a call for broader collaboration and faster progress on shared, polyglot data infrastructure.
key_points: Early pain with Excel/SQL for time series exposed a gap between analytical intent and available tools, driving him to build pandas.
Sustainable open source depends on unglamorous maintenance that corporate incentives often de-prioritize.
Interoperability problems in big-data systems motivated Arrow’s standardized, language-agnostic data format.
Arrow’s goal is reusable, cross-language computational building blocks to reduce duplication and unlock polyglot systems.
Ibis is highlighted as a powerful, underappreciated DSL that improves SQL composability and influences Arrow’s expression work.
money_quotes: It felt like there was a disconnect between the ability of your mind and brain to think about what you want to do with the data and the actual tools to do it.
Doing code reviews and fixing esoteric bugs that people report may not, on paper, seem like a high priority, but really is the core stuff that makes open source software projects successful.
The reason that having this common data format is so important is that it gives you something to collaborate on.

I contend that Ibis is probably one of the most underappreciated things that I've worked on because it makes writing really complex SQL a lot more tractable, and it brings a level of code reuse to SQL analytics that you can't really do in SQL.
The way you reuse code in SQL is by copy and pasting.
I like to tell people that I want the future to get here faster.
It still feels like we're a bit in the wild west in terms of the development of systems and tools for doing data science.
posts.key_insight: Progress in data science tooling requires shared, language-agnostic infrastructure—like Arrow—backed by sustained open-source maintenance rather than isolated, product-driven efforts.

posts.summary: The post is a March 2019 status report from Ursa Labs centered on shipping Apache Arrow 0.13.0 and the infrastructure needed to sustain it. McKinney highlights work on build and test systems, core C++ architecture, and packaging across languages as prerequisites for reliable, scalable development. He frames progress as a mix of foundational engineering (build revamp, expression algebra, query engine design) and ecosystem enablement (Flight, R packaging, Gandiva distribution). The post concludes with gratitude to sponsors and a detailed changelog that signals steady, broad-based momentum. Overall, the argument is that durable data infrastructure requires coordinated, cross-cutting engineering and community collaboration.
key_points: The release focus drove substantial effort on build, test, and packaging infrastructure to keep Arrow development scalable and reliable.
Major C++ work advanced core capabilities like expression algebra and laid groundwork for an embeddable query engine.
Arrow Flight and related tooling progressed through improved dependencies and collaboration with industry partners.
Cross-language support (R, Python, Gandiva, conda-forge) is treated as essential for adoption and stability.
The volume of merged commits and detailed changelog underscores sustained, coordinated progress across the project.
money_quotes: The first quarter of 2019 has now wrapped up.
In March we spent a good amount of time focused on getting the 0.13.0 Apache Arrow release out of the door.
We are continuing to set up our physical build and test cluster.
We have begun prototyping an expression algebra to use for query engine development in C++.
We have been working closely with Two Sigma and Dremio on this important effort.
We are working to get the Arrow R package submitted to CRAN for R users.
We have much work ahead of us and look forward to seeing you on GitHub, JIRA, and the dev@arrow.apache.org developer mailing list.
posts.key_insight: Sustaining Arrow’s growth requires deep infrastructure work across builds, packaging, and core C++ capabilities, coordinated with a broad open-source community.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney argues that pandas succeeded by prioritizing usability over efficiency, but that choice left substantial technical debt that limits scalability. He warns that simply running pandas on clusters is costly because distributed systems add overhead and often fail to beat well-optimized single-node performance. He says the path to affordable scalability is improving data access, single-node performance, and communication costs while avoiding language lock-in. He frames Python’s productivity as essential but insists it should not come with unacceptable performance trade-offs. His conclusion is that Apache Arrow provides the shared, language-agnostic foundation needed to modernize data tooling and end wasteful language wars.
key_points: Pandas was built as a small-data tool, so performance and memory inefficiencies are expected and now constrain scaling.
Distributed computing introduces significant overhead; many systems need large configurations just to beat single-threaded baselines.
Real scalability requires faster data access, better single-node algorithms, and reduced serialization/transfer costs.
Python support in big data systems was often bolted on, making productivity come with excessive performance penalties.
Apache Arrow enables language-independent, high-performance data interchange and a shared foundation for tools like Ray and Dask.
money_quotes: I was willing to write slow, inefficient code if it meant getting something usable out the door that unblocked people's use cases.
Pandas was not designed like a database and was not built by individuals - namely people like myself - with a background in database systems.
Along with making it easier to do distributed computing in Python, we must also improve the single-node computational foundation that projects like Dask and Ray are built on.
It is not enough to simply use these tools to run pandas on a cluster of machines.
This raises the question: why should choosing Python be a trade-off? That doesn't seem right to me.
Together we formed the Apache Arrow project in early 2016 with the mission of creating language-independent standards and computing tools for high-performance in-memory analytics and data transport.

With Arrow now in context, we have an amazing opportunity right now to work together - even across language borders - to build a modern computing foundation to power our data science tools that can even be used from any programming language.
posts.key_insight: Affordable scalability in Python depends on a shared, language-agnostic compute foundation like Apache Arrow, not just distributing existing pandas workloads.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames pandas as the central on-ramp for tabular data in Python and a community-driven open source success without a dominant corporate sponsor. He argues Python’s dominance in data science was not inevitable, but the result of a “perfect storm” of complementary open source projects enabling end-to-end workflows and rapid research-to-production. He describes Apache Arrow as a response to a data interoperability crisis, aiming to eliminate costly data conversion and movement across disparate systems. He explains why traditional database connectors are ill-suited for bulk analytics and how Arrow’s columnar, language-agnostic format solves that bottleneck. He concludes that hardware acceleration and better single-node plus distributed compute architectures are essential, and that current systems like Spark struggle to scale down effectively.
key_points: Pandas became a foundational, community-governed tool for tabular data in Python and underpins most structured data processing.
Python’s rise stemmed from a coordinated ecosystem of open source tools enabling complete workflows and fast deployment.
Arrow was created to address ecosystem-wide interoperability and data-movement costs by standardizing a columnar in-memory format.
ODBC/JDBC are fundamentally mismatched for large-scale analytics due to row orientation and transfer inefficiency.
Future progress depends on hardware acceleration and compute engines that scale efficiently from single-node to distributed.
money_quotes: Pandas serves sort of as a Swiss Army knife for data access, data manipulation, analytics, and data visualization in Python.
It certainly wasn't clear - it wasn't predetermined or inevitable that Python was going to become the number one data language that it is today.
Without scikit-learn, without pandas, without NumPy, it just wouldn't have.
The entire data ecosystem was facing this grand data interchange and data interoperability crisis.
It's kind of like - as a user of these tools, you have this feeling of drinking a thick milkshake through a straw that's too small.
Arrow removes the need for that conversion.

It was definitely serendipitous, but it was also a lot of hard labor to be able to get so many people to agree with each other to build one thing, as opposed to 14 different incompatible "quote unquote" standards.
One of the things that you find with things like Spark is that they fail to shrink down and effectively do computing at the single-node scale.
posts.key_insight: Sustained progress in data science depends on open, interoperable infrastructure like Arrow and compute systems that eliminate data movement costs while scaling efficiently from single-node to distributed environments.

posts.summary: The post argues that incremental, ad hoc collaboration in data infrastructure is not enough to achieve long-term progress, so a unified effort is required. It recounts Apache Arrow’s dual mission—an open standard for columnar interchange and a high-performance analytics foundation—and how separate initiatives like Ursa Labs/Computing and GPU-accelerated analytics (RAPIDS, BlazingSQL) advanced parts of that vision. Arrow has become the dominant interchange format and is extending to faster data access via Flight, but the ecosystem still lacks a single, cohesive push for Arrow-native computing. To address this, the authors announce the formation of Voltron Data, merging teams and expertise to make Arrow a native, hardware-optimized analytics substrate across languages. The conclusion is a call to grow Arrow’s footprint through this new company and its continued open-source stewardship.
key_points: Most collaboration in the data ecosystem stays superficial; meaningful progress needs unified goals and structure.
Apache Arrow’s mission spans both a language-independent data interchange standard and a high-performance analytics foundation.
Parallel GPU analytics efforts proved the value of accelerated columnar computing but only covered parts of the community’s needs.
Arrow is now the gold standard for tabular interchange and is expanding with Flight to replace slow access protocols.
Voltron Data consolidates teams and projects to drive an Arrow-native, hardware-optimized analytics future while supporting open source.
money_quotes: “We realized our best hope to make lasting progress was to build a stronger, unified foundation.”
“The first goal is to be an efficient, language-independent open standard for columnar data interchange.”
“The second goal is to be a portable, high-performance computing foundation for doing analytics on that columnar data.”
“Arrow has been rapidly adopted as the gold standard for tabular data interchange across the data warehousing and data science ecosystems.”
“The next stage of growth is to see Arrow adopted not only as the standard for fast data movement but also as the native format for cost-effective analytical computing.”
“Today, we are launching a new company, Voltron Data, that reflects this unified vision.”
“Together we are unifying our collective expertise in performance, portability, and programmability to build more bridges across the data ecosystem.”
posts.key_insight: Lasting progress in data analytics requires a unified, Arrow-native foundation, which Voltron Data aims to deliver by consolidating open-source expertise and industry momentum.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney argues that Apache Arrow was designed as a language-independent foundation for analytical computing and has become a de facto standard for data interchange across systems and languages. He outlines the project’s growth, including industry funding via Ursa Labs and the formation of Voltron Data to accelerate enterprise adoption and performance across hardware. The talk emphasizes Arrow’s R integration, especially dplyr compatibility, semantic versioning stability, and rapid expansion of supported functions. He highlights performance gains from Arrow’s columnar format, dataset scanning optimizations, and large-scale querying without excessive memory use. He concludes that the next frontier is reducing fragmentation between query frontends and backends via a shared intermediate representation like Substrait to improve composability and interoperability.
key_points: Arrow’s core value is a universal columnar format that enables zero-copy data sharing across languages and systems.
The project’s sustainability strategy evolved from Ursa Labs funding to Voltron Data to drive broader enterprise adoption.
Arrow’s R package focuses on tight dplyr integration, fast iteration via nightly builds, and expanding function coverage.
Performance gains come from Arrow’s memory efficiency and dataset pruning that limits scans and decoding.
A unified compute representation (Substrait) is needed to bridge diverse frontends and backend engines.
money_quotes: The Arrow project was conceived to provide a language independent foundation for analytical computing focused on data frames and tabular data.
Arrow has become one of the de facto, if not the de facto standard technology for connecting external data sources to the data science world.

Rather than having to serialize data every time you move data between a different environment, we have a common data representation that can be shared without any copying or conversion between Java and C++ or Rust and Go or Python or R and JavaScript.
If you're familiar with ODBC or JDBC, we want to replace those more inefficient transfer protocols, interfaces to database systems with one that is Arrow-based.
We analyze these expressions and use them to prune files from having to be scanned or to do filtering within the files to limit the amount of data that we have to decode into Arrow format.
We're able to evaluate this dplyr expression on a 40 gigabyte data set in a little over one second.
You can choose the API that suits you best, and you can drive as many different compute engines as you have available to you without having to significantly rewrite your code.
posts.key_insight: Arrow’s long-term impact comes from standardizing data representation and compute interfaces so users can scale analytics across languages and engines without rewriting code.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames Apache Arrow as a foundational, language-independent infrastructure layer aimed at improving performance and interoperability across the data science ecosystem. He connects Arrow to the pain points that led to pandas, where core analytics required custom, project-specific engines. Arrow’s purpose is to replace that fragmentation with a reusable, modular compute foundation that accelerates both new and existing dataframe tools. He argues open source is essential for adoption and impact because permissive licensing lets other projects and companies depend on Arrow directly. He concludes that future gains depend on unifying software layers that can harness new hardware efficiently while integrating cleanly with languages and storage systems.
key_points: Arrow emerged to provide a future-proof compute foundation that pandas lacked, reducing reinvention across tools.
The project aims to be mostly invisible to users while materially improving performance across many systems.
Permissive open source licensing is the fastest path to ecosystem-wide adoption and impact.
Unlocking new hardware efficiency requires a unified software layer integrated with languages and storage.
money_quotes: I found that I enjoyed building data analysis tools and especially building tools for other people.
One of the things that's interesting about it is for many users, it's a project that they won't even know that's there.
The Arrow project started as an effort to build a more future-proof computing infrastructure for libraries like pandas.
We built effectively a query engine in Python and C to power all of the analytics that pandas performs.
The Arrow project is intended to provide a reusable and modular computing foundation to power the next generation of data frame libraries.
Open-source is extremely important because it's the most efficient way for the software to have an impact.
Open-source really enables that rapid adoption and reduces the barrier to entry.
posts.key_insight: Arrow exists to replace bespoke, project-specific compute stacks with a reusable open-source foundation that makes data tools faster, more interoperable, and ready for next-generation hardware.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames Apache Arrow as a long-term effort to create a universal, language-agnostic foundation for tabular data processing and to reduce friction between systems. He explains that Voltron Data’s mission is to make the analytics stack more modular and composable so developers can better leverage modern hardware. Arrow’s scope has expanded from a data format to a multi-language toolbox that includes serialization, RPC, database interfaces, and compute engines. He emphasizes Substrate as an intermediate representation that lets UI layers and execution engines evolve independently and reduces custom integration work. He concludes that the hardest, most essential part of building sustainable open source is the social dimension, and sees Arrow as a durable agent of progress in the ecosystem.
key_points: Arrow began as a universal tabular data format but has grown into a multi-language stack covering transport, databases, and compute.
Voltron Data unifies teams to build a modular, composable analytics stack that unlocks GPU and hardware acceleration.
Substrate aims to decouple APIs from engines by providing a lower-level IR than SQL for dataframe operations.
Arrow reduced Python-on-Spark overhead dramatically, enabling faster custom code without serialization penalties.
Sustaining large open source projects depends as much on relationships and community trust as on code.
money_quotes: The mission of the company, the heart or the soul of the company is making the modern data analytics stack more modular and composable to make it easier for developers and users of analytics or data engineering tools to unlock the value of modern hardware.
The way that we describe the project these days is we describe the Arrow project as being a multi-language toolbox for building analytical data processing systems.
We were able to make custom code running in PySpark 10 to a 100 times faster in some cases.
You can think about Substrate as being something that's lower level than SQL and can be used to represent tabular data or data frame operations that go outside of what is expressible in SQL.
The lessons learned are that relationships really matter.

The social dimension of building these types of projects is the most difficult, but also the most important if your goal is to build something that has large scope and that you want to be sustainable over a long period of time.
I think the Arrow project as being an agent of change and progress in the open source ecosystem.
I'm looking forward to a day when we won't have to think about that. We'll have written some of our last data connectors, and we can just think about Arrow, and that will make our lives a lot easier.
posts.key_insight: Arrow’s growing ecosystem is about creating a universal, composable foundation for analytics that reduces integration friction and enables sustainable, multi-language data systems.

posts.summary: McKinney reflects on 15 years of building data tools, arguing that early Python data science succeeded but hit limits in performance, scale, and interoperability. He frames these pains as consequences of tightly coupled, vertically integrated systems and the lack of shared standards. Shifts in hardware, heterogeneous workloads, and ML tooling have made modular, composable systems both necessary and feasible. He highlights Arrow, RAPIDS, DuckDB/Velox, Ibis/dplyr, and Substrait as key pieces advancing a “Great Decoupling.” The conclusion is optimistic: commoditized query engines and shared standards will shift effort toward better user interfaces and long‑term open source sustainability.
key_points: pandas and NumPy’s design constraints exposed deeper issues in eager APIs, interoperability, and volunteer-driven sustainability
hardware evolution and heterogeneous pipelines drive the need for modular, reusable components rather than monolithic systems
Apache Arrow enables a common in-memory format and compute layer, reducing interop cost and enabling hardware-optimized kernels
embeddable query engines (DuckDB, Velox, DataFusion) are commoditizing OLAP execution and making advanced SQL ubiquitous
Substrait and portable APIs (Ibis/dplyr) aim to decouple frontends from backends and reduce SQL dialect fragmentation
money_quotes: fragmentation is killing us.
pandas had to do everything for itself, and this is an enormous burden for a fully volunteer-based open source project.
Eagerly-evaluated APIs (as opposed to “lazy” ones) make it more difficult to do efficient “query” planning and execution.
I believe now more than ever that “the future is Arrow-native.”
The ability to add cutting-edge analytic SQL processing to almost any application is a disruptive and transformative change for our industry.
I believe he’s right and this is a big deal.
I’m optimistic about what the future holds for the continuing trends of modularization, interoperability, and composability, and what I dubbed the “Great Decoupling.”
Lastly, as developer energy shifts away from “who can build the fastest execution engine”, I predict the coming years will bring a new wave of investment in user interface productivity.
posts.key_insight: The path forward for data systems is a modular, Arrow-centered stack where interoperable components commoditize execution and free developers to focus on better, portable user interfaces.

posts.summary: McKinney announces a transition out of his full-time CTO role at Voltron Data to broaden his work across entrepreneurial and open source data projects while staying on as Senior Advisor. He recounts the arc from Apache Arrow and Ibis in 2015 through Ursa Labs and Ursa Computing to founding Voltron Data and raising major capital to invest in the composable data stack. The post emphasizes how interoperability, composability, and polyglot collaboration shaped funding and partnerships with groups like Two Sigma, NVIDIA, and Posit. He argues that scaling startups require focus, and his strongest contributions are in early-stage vision, team formation, and ecosystem building. He concludes with optimism about the road ahead and continued involvement in high-impact projects and advising startups.
key_points: Early funding and institutional support were decisive in turning Arrow from a spec into a thriving, extensible ecosystem.
Interoperability and composability across languages and systems are the central architectural goals driving his work.
Voltron Data’s growth and investments reflect a strategic bet on the composable data stack and Arrow-centric tooling.
He prefers early-stage startup phases and steps aside to enable new leaders once projects mature.
He plans to keep catalyzing ecosystem projects while maintaining improved work-life balance.
money_quotes: I am transitioning out of my full-time CTO role at Voltron Data so that I can expand my portfolio of entrepreneurial and open source data projects.
Two Sigma had already been incorporating these ideas in their systems since the late 2000s.
We all agreed that the “language wars” between Python and R were counterproductive, and Posit recognized that the future of data science is polyglot.
All startups as they transition from early- to mid-stage must focus on specific areas of product development and revenue growth in order to achieve long-term viability.

Personally, I have always been energized by the early stages of projects: identifying a problem, working really hard to assemble a team and bootstrap initial solutions, and then getting the project to a point where I don’t have to be involved 100% of the time, so others have an opportunity to step up and become leaders.
If I were to remain in a long-term operational role, it might limit my ability to pursue other impactful projects that fall too far outside of Voltron Data’s wheelhouse.
There is yet a long road ahead of us.
posts.key_insight: He is stepping back from day-to-day leadership at Voltron Data because his highest leverage lies in seeding interoperable, composable data ecosystems and catalyzing new projects.

posts.summary: McKinney announces he is joining Posit as Principal Architect to advocate for the PyData ecosystem and advance open source efforts aligned with a polyglot data science mission. He traces his long relationship with Posit’s leadership back to 2012, emphasizing early shared goals of open source adoption and cross-language collaboration. A central argument is that sustainable open source requires careful funding models and aligned commercial structures, and Posit is a rare example that has balanced business success with community stewardship. He links his work on Arrow, Feather, and interoperability standards to this broader vision of composable, reusable infrastructure. The conclusion is that Posit’s mission, governance, and growth model make it the best place for him to sustain long-term impact in open source data science.
key_points: R and Python communities benefit more from collaboration and interoperability than competition, especially against closed-source alternatives.
Sustaining open source over the long term is a larger challenge than the technology itself, often dominated by funding and burnout concerns.
Posit’s bootstrap origins, business leadership, and dedicated open source stewardship enabled a healthier model than typical VC- or consulting-driven paths.
Cross-language standards like Arrow and Jupyter are essential to a composable, polyglot data science stack.
McKinney’s move to Posit reflects a strategic choice to maximize long-term open source impact.
money_quotes: Building open source sustainably over a long period of time is difficult, and I’ve found that when I meet with other open source developers we often spend much more time talking about project sustainability, funding strategies, and maintainer burnout as opposed to technology problems we want to solve or other aspirational project goals.
Posit is a rare example of a company that has managed to build a commercially successful business while maintaining a healthy relationship with the open source communities that it supports.
Conflicts often emerge pitting the interests of the open source projects and communities against the need to generate revenue and become profitable.

To do that, we knew we needed to develop new language-independent computing frameworks like Arrow and interoperability standards like the Jupyter kernel protocol which could enable the development of portable infrastructure for data science.
Multi-language technology for data science has come a long way since my initial forays in open source in 2009 with pandas, and in many ways it still feels like we are just getting started.

Just as Arrow has brought about a unified cross-language computing layer for data analytics, projects like Jupyter and more recently Quarto have had a similar impact on interactive computing and technical publishing, respectively.

I believe the company’s mantras of technical excellence, sustainable growth, and supporting the public good position it to be a force for progress and innovation in the data science world for many years to come.
posts.key_insight: Posit’s mission and business model align uniquely well with the long-term, interoperable open source infrastructure McKinney wants to build across languages.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney reflects on how pandas began as a push to make basic data work possible in Python and argues that the field has matured into concerns like interoperability, scalability, and performance. He makes the case for composable systems over vertically integrated stacks, noting that collaboration and shared standards are the only path to break repeated interoperability walls. Apache Arrow is presented as the key enabler that removes serialization overhead while also improving single-system performance through columnar memory layouts. He describes pandas’ gradual Arrow integration and why backward compatibility forces a deliberate migration. He positions Ibis as a portable, SQL-compatible query layer that turns SQL into machine-generated assembly rather than a human interface. He concludes that sustainable open source hinges on social collaboration and that the real divide is open source versus closed source, not language wars.
key_points: Early pandas aimed at basic Python data work; today’s challenges are interoperability, performance, and scalability.
Composable systems require shared standards; Arrow solves cross-system data exchange while staying efficient in-memory.
pandas 2.0 integrates Arrow types for better memory use and nullability but must move carefully to avoid breakage.
Ibis provides a Pythonic, backend-agnostic query layer with strong SQL compatibility and validation.
Open source success is primarily a social challenge, and collaboration across languages is essential.
money_quotes: At that time, I was really concerned with just making Python a language where you could succeed at doing really basic data work.
Clearly the future should look like we have fewer components, we have a lot more data, but the data is being processed by a lot less code.
Often, that's the hardest part of building the software projects—the social side.
The hard problems in open source projects are not necessarily technical problems—they're actually social problems—building relationships with people.
If you can do it in SQL, there should be a way to express the same thing in Ibis.
Honestly, SQL is not going anywhere. And I think the best thing that we can do is turn SQL into basically the assembly language of databases.
We stand to gain more from working together and sharing ideas and if possible, sharing code and solutions to problems than competing with each other.
The struggle is not R versus Python. It's open source versus closed source.
posts.key_insight: Composable data systems only scale when communities standardize and collaborate, with Arrow and Ibis exemplifying how shared infrastructure beats isolated stacks.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney recounts how pandas emerged from urgent needs in quant finance, evolved through open-source community building, and matured as he stepped back from leadership. He argues that pragmatic usability and interoperability drove pandas’ early design, while long-term maintenance now requires balancing backwards compatibility against structural improvements. He emphasizes that sustainable open source depends on public communication, inclusive governance, and durable funding models rather than individual heroics. He frames Apache-style transparency and corporate collaboration as essential to scaling infrastructure like Arrow and enabling cross-language interoperability. He concludes that open source is now intertwined with business strategy, and the AI era risks concentrating power unless openness, funding, and access to compute improve.
key_points: pandas grew from a practical need for easier, one-line data manipulation, with pragmatism and usability guiding its design.
Large open-source projects must balance backwards compatibility with necessary architectural changes, especially as ecosystems evolve.
Transparent, public governance protects communities from corporate capture and enables inclusive collaboration.
Sustainable open source requires funding and paid contributors; relying on personal heroics is not viable long term.
In AI, open frameworks may remain open while proprietary models and cloud platforms concentrate power and extract rents.
money_quotes: I really developed this mindset of everything must be really easy. If we can get this down to one line of code that's easy to write, easy to reason about.

I think it's a sign of maturity and progress in an open source project when the creator is able to take a step back and not have to loosen your grip and allow other people to step in and step into a leadership role.
One of the things that really helps is just making sure that as much of your project communication as possible is happening in public.
Open source was supposed to be about freedom, freedom to understand, freedom to modify.
Open source has become a go-to-market strategy.
The goal is to create products and services that are so essential that a large fraction of the world is like their serfs.
How am I supposed to have a life and a family and stuff if I'm working 80 or 100 hours a week for the rest of my life to engage in these open source heroics?
Moving from a more federated, fragmented ecosystem to one that's more like working together and more focused on usability and user experience.
posts.key_insight: Open source succeeds when it pairs pragmatic design and public governance with sustainable funding, but the AI era threatens to centralize power unless openness and access to compute improve.

posts.summary: Wes argues that data systems should be built as composable stacks of open standards, modular engines, and shared protocols rather than vertically integrated monoliths. He explains why this shift is happening now: cloud-era standards like Parquet, faster hardware, and the limits of earlier Hadoop-era systems make interoperability feasible and necessary. He highlights Apache Arrow, ADBC/Flight, modular execution engines, and Substrait as the technical backbone enabling a multi-engine future. He frames composability as a way to reduce distributed-system overheads, improve performance portability, and adapt to new hardware accelerators. The conclusion is a vision where users can seamlessly move from laptop-scale workflows to large-scale clusters without rewriting queries or changing tools, with Arrow becoming a de facto connective standard.
key_points: Composable systems rely on open standards and modular components to avoid the inefficiencies and lock-in of vertically integrated stacks.
Historical shifts—cloud delivery, open formats, and hardware advances—make composability practical in the 2020s.
Apache Arrow and related protocols solve cross-language data interchange and database export bottlenecks.
Substrait and modular engines decouple language frontends from execution backends, enabling multi-engine portability.
A unified dev-to-prod workflow (e.g., DuckDB to Snowflake/Spark) is the practical payoff of the multi-engine vision.
money_quotes: Hadley and I think that the language wars are stupid. All the enmity between the R and Python communities is counterproductive.
We are building systems based on open standards and protocols.

The hope is that over time, each of the component pieces that we use to build these systems becomes so much better, easier to use, faster, more interoperable, and that you end up creating a solution that is a lot more future-proof.
To what degree are these systems truly improving performance, as opposed to parallelizing the overheads that they introduce?
If you need to export a lot of data out of a database, you're trying to drink a thick milkshake through a straw that's too small.
In many cases, a terabyte is no longer big data, and so for many people, big data is indeed dead. But there still is big data.
Nobody wants to burn a ton of Snowflake credits while they're developing their ETL workflow.
Now almost eight years into Arrow... it's great that Arrow is becoming the de facto standard for how we build APIs and how we connect systems together.
posts.key_insight: Composable, standards-based data systems are the path to portable performance, letting users move across engines and scales without sacrificing productivity or interoperability.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames his career around building long-lived, sustainable data infrastructure, from pandas to Arrow and Ibis, with a focus on composability across languages and systems. He argues that open source success depends as much on social coordination and governance as on technical design, and that creating space for new maintainers is essential for project longevity. He highlights Apache Arrow as a standardized, language-independent in-memory format that enables zero-copy interoperability and faster, reusable algorithms across ecosystems. He sees WebAssembly and tools like DuckDB as transformative for running high-performance analytics in the browser without servers. He positions Ibis and related tools as enabling a multi-engine data stack where developers can move between local and scalable backends without rewriting code. The conclusion is that the ecosystem has made huge progress but is still early, with significant opportunity to keep simplifying and accelerating data work.
key_points: Open source sustainability and shared ownership are critical; social coordination can be harder than technical engineering.
Arrow provides a universal, efficient data representation that unlocks cross-language interoperability and reusable computing components.
WebAssembly enables full scientific Python stacks and analytics engines to run client-side, changing deployment models.
Ibis and similar abstractions promote a multi-engine data stack, allowing portability across local and distributed systems.
pandas succeeded by prioritizing simplicity and lowering barriers, which amplified community adoption and skill portability.
money_quotes: It's really refreshing to work with people who are truly mission focused and want to bring open source data science tools to everyone, sustainably and for the long term.
I've spoken a lot about how important that is—for open source project creators to make room for others in steering and growing the project so they can become owners as well.
It turns out that, as is true with many open source software problems, the social problems are harder than the technical problems.
If you were building pandas now, you could build a pandas-like library based on Arrow components in much less time.
WebAssembly has opened up this whole new world of possibilities and is transformative, I think.
The mantra in pandas was: how do we make things one line of code? One line of code—it must be easy.
I think one reason pandas has gotten so popular is that it's beneficial to the community to have fewer solutions.
There's still a lot to do. We've made a lot of progress in the last 15 plus years, but in some ways it feels like we're just getting started.
posts.key_insight: The long-term impact in data science comes from building interoperable infrastructure and sustainable open source communities that make powerful capabilities simple and portable.

posts.summary: McKinney argues that the meaning of “big data” has shifted as hardware capabilities have exploded, making many workloads that once required clusters feasible on a single machine. He critiques the historical big data ecosystem for prioritizing scale over ergonomics and for adding heavy overhead that raises per‑unit processing costs. The talk frames modern data systems around modularity, composability, and interoperability, enabled by open standards like Apache Arrow and protocols such as Arrow Flight. He highlights the rise of embeddable, high‑performance engines (e.g., DuckDB, Polars, DataFusion, Velox) and the importance of decoupling query interfaces from execution and storage. The conclusion is a vision of a multi‑engine data stack where users choose the best runtime for data size and workload while keeping flexible language front ends.
key_points: Hardware advances have collapsed the gap between “big data” and what fits on a laptop, changing system design priorities.
Many distributed systems achieved scalability by parallelizing overhead, often at a high cost per GB/TB.
Composability through open standards (Arrow, Parquet, Iceberg, lineage metadata) enables efficient multi‑system stacks.
Embedding and reusing execution engines (DuckDB, Polars, DataFusion, Velox) reduces redundant engine building.
Query‑layer abstraction projects (Ibis, Narwhals, Modin) allow one API to target many backends and data scales.
money_quotes: What used to be big data can now fit on your laptop.
In 2004, all servers and data centers had a single processor core.
These systems have achieved impressive scalability, but to what extent are they truly improving performance as opposed to parallelizing the overhead that they introduce into the process.

Wouldn't it be amazing if we had a powerful computational runtime that could be shared across programming languages so that it isn't all the Python developers working for themselves to rebuild everything just for Python.
DuckDB... has pretty much shut down the cottage industry of people building their own database and data processing engines.
Why be locked into a single execution engine, along with a full stack of tools, including storage, and query interface.

The hope of all this is to work towards a future multi engine data stack where we can choose the execution engine that makes most sense for the size of data to get good performance and good efficiency.
posts.key_insight: The future of data systems is a modular, open‑standard, multi‑engine stack that lets users pick the right runtime for their workload without being locked into a single toolchain.

posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames his move to Posit as a continuation of long-standing collaboration aimed at building polyglot, open-source data tools. He argues that the real competition is open source versus proprietary software, not R versus Python, and that productive collaboration grows once language wars subside. He highlights persistent pain around cross-language packaging and deployment, pointing to tools like Conda, Mamba, and Pixi as partial remedies but not a full solution. On AI, he treats Copilot integration as table stakes while warning about ethical and workforce impacts, and he is interested in LLMs as research assistants for data exploration. His career advice centers on long-term relationship building and resilience to negative feedback in open source.
key_points: Posit’s role in incubating Arrow and related efforts shows the value of cross-language collaboration and shared infrastructure.
Packaging and deployment across Python and R remain major blockers; language-agnostic tools are necessary but still immature.
The most important strategic divide is open source versus closed source, not language rivalry.
LLM tooling should augment workflows beyond code generation, but raises ethical and labor concerns.
Sustainable careers in open source require long-term relationship investment and emotional resilience.
money_quotes: We’re not working R versus Python, we’re really open source versus closed source proprietary software.

Getting past the red team, blue team bickering about whose programming language is better has enabled us to have just a lot more productive conversations about how we can build real tools for humans that are usable and accessible.
The feedback that you get in an open-source project tends to only be negative feedback.
When somebody’s annoyed with your software not doing what they wish it did or being perfect in the ways that they wish for it to be perfect, that comment is as much about them as it is about you.
The downside of making developers more productive is in the future we need fewer developers, most likely.
I’ve made a personal promise to myself to never work on a Python packaging tool because we’ve all thought about it.
Focus on people and relationships, investing in your relationships with people that you want to work with for a long time and also viewing your working relationships with a long-term mindset.
posts.key_insight: The long-term leverage in data tooling comes from open, interoperable infrastructure and sustained human relationships, not language tribalism.

posts.summary: In this DealMakers interview, Wes McKinney recounts his path from math competitions to building pandas at AQR as an unsanctioned skunkworks project that ultimately helped drive Python’s dominance in data science. He explains the case for open-sourcing pandas, emphasizing the benefits of external contributions and long-term ecosystem growth. The conversation traces his startup journey through Datapad, the creation of Apache Arrow, and the formation of Voltron Data, highlighting how market evidence and conviction shaped his decisions. He describes stepping back from an operational role as Voltron scaled and the macro environment shifted, to focus on a broader portfolio and investing. He closes with investing and founder advice centered on composability, technical leadership, and long-term integrity in relationships.
key_points: Open-sourcing pandas was a strategic bet that paid off by accelerating Python’s data ecosystem and industry adoption.
Founding decisions should be driven by real market evidence and conviction, not startup momentum alone.
Apache Arrow emerged as a foundational interoperability layer, leading to consortium funding and later a venture-backed company.
Voltron’s rapid scaling and changing capital conditions required sharper focus, prompting a shift in Wes’s role.
His investing thesis targets a composable, interoperable data stack led by technical founders who can execute.
money_quotes: It was totally a skunkworks project. It wasn't really sanctioned, authorized, or planned by company management.
It required a leap of faith on everyone's part, but I think in the fullness of time, it's been a good decision.
Without pandas and the other projects that happened at the same time, it's unclear that Python would be where it is today as the number-one language of AI and data science.
